skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: A systematic debugging methodology that finds root causes before proposing fixes, preventing wasted time on symptom-based solutions
skill.summary: Four-phase debugging framework: investigate root cause, analyze patterns, test hypotheses, implement verified fixes
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 126 patterns but 125 are false positives from markdown documentation (backticks are code formatting, not shell execution). One legitimate shell script (find-polluter.sh) uses npm test for test debugging - acceptable for a debugging skill. No cryptographic code exists despite scanner claims. Skill teaches debugging methodology safely.

low_findings.title: Shell Script for Test Debugging
low_findings.description: find-polluter.sh executes npm test commands to identify which test creates unwanted files or state. This is legitimate test infrastructure for a debugging skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Script purpose is clearly documented for test pollution debugging - npm test is standard development workflow, not malicious.

content.user_title: Debug systematically to find root causes before fixing
content.value_statement: Random fixes waste time and create new bugs. This skill provides a four-phase debugging framework that finds root causes before implementing solutions, preventing wasted effort on symptom-based fixes.
actual_capabilities: Guides through four-phase debugging process: investigation, pattern analysis, hypothesis testing, implementation
Teaches backward tracing through call stacks to find original bug triggers
Provides defense-in-depth validation strategies for multi-layer systems
Replaces flaky timeout-based tests with condition-based waiting patterns
Includes test pollution detection scripts for finding state-leaking tests
limitations: Focuses on debugging methodology, not domain-specific debugging knowledge
Requires user to run diagnostic commands - cannot execute code directly
Best suited for software bugs, not hardware or network infrastructure issues
Assumes access to source code and version control for change analysis

use_cases.title: Test Failure Investigation
use_cases.description: When tests fail intermittently or unexpectedly, use this skill to systematically trace the failure back to its root cause rather than applying quick fixes.

use_cases.title: Production Bug Resolution
use_cases.description: When bugs appear in production, follow the four-phase process to understand the root cause before deploying fixes that might create new issues.

use_cases.title: Multi-Component Debugging
use_cases.description: When debugging complex systems with multiple layers, use defense-in-depth diagnostics to identify exactly which component is failing.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers working with layered architectures

prompt_templates.title: Basic Bug Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm seeing this error: [paste error]. I want to fix it quickly, but I know I should investigate first. Help me follow Phase 1 of systematic debugging to understand the root cause before proposing any fixes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner encountering a clear error message

prompt_templates.title: Flaky Test Debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: This test fails intermittently: [paste test]. Sometimes it passes, sometimes it fails. Guide me through systematic debugging to find what's causing the flakiness, including how to add diagnostic instrumentation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer dealing with non-deterministic test failures

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Layer System Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: My system has [describe layers: e.g., CI workflow, build script, signing process]. Something is breaking but I don't know which layer. Show me how to add evidence-gathering instrumentation at each boundary to isolate the failing component.
prompt_templates.scenario: Debugging complex systems with multiple components

prompt_templates.title: Failed Fix Recovery
prompt_templates.prompt: I've tried [N] fixes already and none worked. Each fix revealed a new problem. Help me stop and question whether there's an architectural issue before attempting another fix. Guide me through re-analyzing from Phase 1.
prompt_templates.scenario: After multiple failed fix attempts, questioning the approach

output_examples.input: Test fails with git init error in wrong directory
output: 1. Error shows git init running in /source/code not /tmp
2. Traced backward: git init <- WorktreeManager <- Session.create <- test
3. Found: projectDir was empty string, resolved to process.cwd()
4. Root cause: test accessed context.tempDir before beforeEach initialized it
Root cause identified: variable initialization order issue

output_examples.input: Flaky test that sometimes misses tool results
output: Diagnosis: Test uses arbitrary setTimeout waits instead of condition checking
Solution: Replace timeouts with condition-based waiting

best_practices: Always complete Phase 1 investigation before proposing any fix - understanding the root cause is faster than thrashing with random fixes
Trace bugs backward through the call stack to find the original trigger, not just where the error appears
After 3 failed fix attempts, stop and question the architecture rather than attempting more symptom fixes
anti_patterns: Proposing fixes before completing root cause investigation - this treats symptoms, not causes
Making multiple changes at once - you cannot isolate what worked and may introduce new bugs
Skipping the failing test creation - untested fixes do not stick and regressions go unnoticed

faq.question: Isn't systematic debugging slower than just trying quick fixes?
faq.answer: No. Studies from actual sessions show systematic debugging takes 15-30 minutes versus 2-3 hours of thrashing with random fixes. The first-time fix rate is 95% versus 40%.

faq.question: What if I'm in an emergency and need a fix now?
faq.answer: Emergencies make guessing tempting but systematic is still faster. Rushing guarantees rework. The process is designed to resist time pressure rationalization.

faq.question: How do I know when I've found the real root cause?
faq.answer: You can trace the complete chain from symptom back to source. If you fix at the source and add validation at each layer, the bug becomes impossible.

faq.question: What if my first hypothesis is wrong?
faq.answer: That's expected. Form a single hypothesis, test it minimally, and if it fails, return to Phase 1 with new information. Never add more fixes on top of failed ones.

faq.question: When should I question the architecture versus keep debugging?
faq.answer: After 3 failed fix attempts where each reveals new problems in different places, stop. This indicates architectural issues, not just bugs. Discuss refactoring before more fixes.

faq.question: Does this work for non-code bugs like configuration issues?
faq.answer: Yes. The four-phase process applies to any technical problem: reproduce, gather evidence, trace data flow, identify differences from working configurations.
